items.q: Why do more massive stars have shorter main-sequence lifetimes than smaller ones?
items.correct: They burn through their hydrogen fuel much faster because higher core temperature drives nuclear fusion at a vastly greater rate.
items.misconception: All stars live the same time on the main sequence regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: More massive stars live longer because they have more fuel.
items.why: Students assume more fuel always means longer life, missing that mass increases consumption rate even more sharply than fuel supply.

items.q: Why do brighter-looking stars not necessarily have higher luminosity?
items.correct: Apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance — a closer dim star can look brighter than a distant luminous one.
items.misconception: Brighter-looking stars are always intrinsically more luminous.
items.random_wrong: Apparent brightness depends only on the star's temperature.
items.why: Students don't naturally factor in distance; they assume 'looks bright' means 'is bright', missing the inverse-square law applied to flux.

items.q: Do all stars end their lives as supernovae?
items.correct: No — only sufficiently massive stars (above about 8 solar masses) explode as supernovae; lower-mass stars become white dwarfs.
items.random_wrong: Yes — but only after billions of years on Earth's scale.
items.why: Students learn 'star death' as one storyline and don't separate the different end states based on initial mass.

items.q: On an HR diagram, the main sequence is a:
items.why: It's Diagonal band top-left to bottom-right. “Horizontal line” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: White dwarfs sit on the HR diagram:
items.why: It's Bottom left. “Top right” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 5800. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
